source: Centenarian Coach Nutrition Notes — Protein for Older Adults
content: Adults over 65 need more dietary protein than younger adults to offset age-related anabolic resistance and preserve muscle mass. A common target is 1.2 to 1.6 grams of protein per kilogram of body weight per day, compared with the 0.8 g/kg baseline for younger adults. Spreading intake across meals — roughly 25 to 35 grams per meal — helps maximize muscle protein synthesis.

source: Centenarian Coach Nutrition Notes — Leucine and Muscle Synthesis
content: Leucine is the amino acid that most strongly triggers muscle protein synthesis. Older adults benefit from meals containing 2.5 to 3 grams of leucine, which corresponds to about 25 to 30 grams of high-quality protein from sources such as eggs, dairy, fish, poultry, or soy. Pairing protein intake with resistance training amplifies the muscle-preserving effect.

source: Centenarian Coach Nutrition Notes — Sustainable Calorie Deficits
content: A moderate calorie deficit of 300 to 500 calories per day produces gradual, sustainable fat loss of roughly 0.25 to 0.5 kg per week. Larger deficits accelerate weight loss but increase the risk of muscle loss and rebound. Keeping protein high during a deficit protects lean mass, and prioritizing whole foods over ultra-processed options makes the deficit easier to sustain.

source: Centenarian Coach Nutrition Notes — Foods That Support a Deficit
content: High-satiety, lower-calorie foods make a calorie deficit easier to maintain. Lean proteins, non-starchy vegetables, legumes, fruit, and high-fiber whole grains provide volume and fullness per calorie. Soups, salads, and broth-based meals are especially filling. Limiting calorie-dense ultra-processed snacks and sugary drinks frees up calories without increasing hunger.

source: Centenarian Coach Nutrition Notes — Fiber and Satiety
content: Dietary fiber slows digestion, blunts blood-sugar spikes, and increases satiety, which supports appetite control during weight loss. Most adults should aim for 25 to 38 grams of fiber per day from vegetables, fruit, legumes, nuts, and whole grains. Fiber also feeds beneficial gut bacteria that produce short-chain fatty acids linked to metabolic and immune health.

source: Centenarian Coach Nutrition Notes — Time-Restricted Eating
content: Time-restricted eating confines daily food intake to a window of roughly 8 to 10 hours. Evidence suggests it can support modest weight loss and improved insulin sensitivity, largely by reducing overall calorie intake and aligning eating with circadian rhythms. Direct human longevity benefits are not yet proven, and older adults should ensure the eating window still allows adequate protein and total calories.

source: Centenarian Coach Nutrition Notes — Fasting and Longevity Evidence
content: Caloric restriction and intermittent fasting extend lifespan in many animal models, and short-term human studies show improvements in metabolic markers such as blood glucose, blood pressure, and inflammation. However, long-term human lifespan data are lacking. For older adults, the risk of undernutrition and muscle loss can outweigh benefits, so any fasting protocol should preserve protein intake and be applied cautiously.

source: Centenarian Coach Nutrition Notes — Mediterranean Eating Pattern
content: The Mediterranean dietary pattern — rich in vegetables, fruit, legumes, nuts, whole grains, olive oil, and fish, with limited red and processed meat — is consistently associated with lower cardiovascular disease risk and greater healthy life expectancy. Its emphasis on unsaturated fats, fiber, and polyphenols makes it a strong default pattern for longevity-focused eating.

source: Centenarian Coach Nutrition Notes — Hydration and Micronutrients
content: Older adults have a blunted thirst response and are prone to dehydration; a practical target is 1.5 to 2 liters of fluid per day, adjusted for activity and climate. Micronutrients of particular concern with age include vitamin B12, vitamin D, calcium, and magnesium. A varied whole-food diet plus targeted supplementation where blood work shows a deficiency covers most needs.
